cards.back: A temporary endeavour undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result. Has defined start/end, constrained by scope, time, cost, and produces unique outputs.
Ongoing, repetitive activities that maintain the organization. No defined end, produce standardized outputs, and do not change the organization.
Scope (what work), Time (how long), and Cost (budget) are the three constraints. Quality is not a constraint but the outcome of balancing these three.
A collection of programs and/or projects within an organization, which may be unrelated, grouped to facilitate effective management.
A group of related projects managed together as a coordinated whole to deliver strategic benefit beyond what individual projects could achieve.

cards.front: Name the four stages of the Project Life Cycle.
cards.back: 1. Defining (5-10% effort) 2. Planning (10-20% effort) 3. Executing (60-70% effort) 4. Closing (5-10% effort)

cards.front: Which stage has the highest effort?
cards.back: Executing is the longest and most resource-intensive stage where the actual work happens and deliverables are produced.
The first, shortest stage where the project need is identified, business case developed, and project charter established.
Develop detailed plans for scope, schedule, budget. Identify risks, plan resources, and create the baseline for execution.
Verify deliverables meet requirements, obtain customer acceptance, close contracts, release resources, capture lessons learned.
Early in projects, risk likelihood is HIGH but cost to fix is LOW. Late in projects, likelihood is LOW but cost is HIGH. Early identification is critical.
Projects are the vehicle for achieving organizational strategy. Without formal PM, strategies remain as intentions and never materialize.
